data.content: I can see the project — the express test expects `15.00m` but the method returns `10.00m`, so that one will fail. Let me start `dotnet watch test` so your tests re-run automatically on save:

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test with hot reload

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Start dotnet watch test with hot reload

data.reasoningText:
The user wants to use hot reload to iterate on their failing tests. I can see the express test expects 15.00m but the method returns 10.00m, so that test will fail.

They want me to set up `dotnet watch test` so they can iterate with hot reload. Let me start that up.

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test with hot reload

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-aa6a5e24f92643039d6d0694bd172f7a && dotnet watch test --no-restore 2>&1", "description": "Start dotnet watch test with hot reload", "mode": "async", "shellId": "watch"}

data.content: The watcher started but the test output was sparse. Let me check a normal test run first, then restart the watcher properly:
data.reasoningText:
It seems like `dotnet watch test` ran but the output was minimal. Let me check if the restore/build happened. It seems it exited quickly — possibly because the tests ran and failed. Let me try running without `--no-restore` or check what happened. Actually, let me re-read the output more carefully.

The output shows:
```
dotnet watch ⌚ Waiting for changes
dotnet watch ⌚ Exited
dotnet watch ⏳ Waiting for a file to change before restarting ...
```

It seems like it ran the tests and exited, and now it's waiting for file changes. 

The test output wasn't captured in what I'm seeing, so let me stop this and try running `dotnet test` directly to see the actual failure output clearly.

wait $! 2>/dev/null || true
